1.name: Starting Conditions
prompts: What is the starting atmosphere of this challenge?
What does standing ask of me today?
Where does the body already know how to practice — and where can I trust it to figure out the rest?
What assumptions am I running about how the body should feel?
What did I enjoy most today?

2.name: Support
prompts: What is the ground already giving?
Where am I resisting gravity?
What happens when I let support become more obvious?
What part of the body feels most trustworthy today? and what is it like to rest there?

3.name: Small Dance
prompts: Where is the small dance easiest to find?
Where is the small dance faint, and where could I spend more time looking?
What changes when more joints are included?
What does the small dance teach about posture today?

4.name: Energy
prompts: Describe the experience of moving energy
Where does sensation stream, pulse, spread, or gather?
What is the difference between sensation and interpretation today?
What happens when energy is allowed to move without being managed?

5.name: Friction
prompts: What is the main friction in practice today?
What does friction reveal that ease would hide?
What happens when I include my feelings about the practice?

6.name: Enjoyment
prompts: What was enjoyable, even slightly?
What conditions made practice more interesting?
Where did pleasure appear without being chased?
What does the body want more of?

7.name: Precision
prompts: What coordination demanded precision today?
What did precision teach me today?

8.name: Standing
prompts: Where do I hold myself up unnecessarily?
What does standing make available that sitting does not?

9.name: Namkhor
prompts: Which movement feels most alive right now?
What is the elemental quality of today's practice?
What does the movement reveal about space?
What remains after the movement stops?

10.name: Daily Life Transfer
prompts: Where did practice show up outside formal practice?
What ordinary activity became a practice context?
What changed in walking, sitting, working, or speaking?
What did I remember without prompting?
What does the body now know how to find?

11.name: View
prompts: What did practice show about effort and allowing?

12.name: Integration
prompts: What has changed since Day 1?
Which practice thread wants to continue?
What did this challenge teach me about discipline?

formatBound.text: A letter to your body on the morning of Day 1 — what you're asking it to do, and what you're asking it not to do.
A floor plan of where you stood today. Mark the cardinal directions. X marks the spot.
One photo of where the small dance was loudest today. One sentence underneath.
Today's energy as a weather forecast — temperature, conditions, wind, the long-range outlook.
An itemized receipt for today's practice. Line items, costs, total.
A recipe for the part of practice that worked today. Ingredients and method.
Today's practice as field notes — clinical, third-person, no metaphor. Observe yourself like a stranger in a clearing.
A creature, spell, or land that names what standing made possible today. Cost, type, text, flavor.
Three to five color swatches with tiny labels — the colors of today's namkhor. "The tan of the carpet at minute 14."
Today's practice as a single footnote — citing the books, songs, places, people, or memories that showed up.
Write a long reflection. Then black out everything except what is still true an hour later.
An AI image prompt that encapsulates the joys and difficulties of these sixty days. One image. One prompt.
Six seconds of video. The smallest unit of today's practice that still shows what it was.
Fifteen seconds, locked camera, no cut. The shot of you not trying to be on camera.
Today at 60× speed. One minute of video for sixty minutes of practice.
Record yourself guiding yourself through the whole practice. Talk it through. The audio + transcript is the entry.
After practice, sit with the recorder. Talk for as long as you have to. Stop when you stop.
Handwrite today's entry. Photograph the page. The handwriting is the entry.
What song is this practice today? Embed it. One line about why.
Today's practice referenced — knowingly or not — these things. Cite them like a syllabus.

constraint: Today: a single sentence. No semicolons.
